sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:

Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: - The green 2x2 dot (id=34) is the player/agent that moves with arrow keys - It started inside a gray box at bottom-left and moved RIGHT by 2 columns - The gray box seems to be a starting container - Right side has two cross-shaped targets (red top, blue bottom) - Divider at col 32-33 with white dots Goal hypothesis: Navigate the green dot from left side to one of the right-side targets
- Plan: Try moving green dot UP to exit dark red box from top
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 78 actions; a strong score needs about 59 or fewer. You are 1.3x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The green dot is still at [30,20]-[31,21], even after 6 UP actions. This means the green dot is blocked from moving UP. The dark red box is at [30,18]-[33,21], and the green dot is at [30,20]-[31,21], which is at the top of the box.
The green dot can't move UP because it's blocked by the top edge of the dark red box. Similarly, it can't move RIGHT because it's blocked by the right edge. It can move LEFT into the red bar, and it can move DOWN within the dark red box.
So the green dot is trapped inside the dark red box, with only LEFT and DOWN movements possible (and DOWN is limited by the box's bottom edge).
Wait, but earlier the green dot was able to exit the gray box by moving UP. Let me check if the gray box and dark red box have different properties.
Actually, looking at the image, the green dot is at [30,20]-[31,21], which is at the top of the dark red box. The dark red box spans rows 30-33. The green dot can't move UP because it's at the top edge of the box.
But wait, the green dot was at [32,20]-[33,21] before, and it moved UP to [30,20]-[31,21]. So the green dot moved 2 rows UP (1 UP action). Then I sent 6 more UP actions, but the green dot didn't move further. This means the green dot is blocked at the top edge of the dark red box.
So the green dot can only move within the dark red box, and it can exit by moving LEFT into the red bar. But it can't exit UP, DOWN, or RIGHT.
This is frustrating. The green dot is trapped inside the dark red box, and I can't get it to the right side targets.
Let me reconsider the problem. Maybe the goal is not to navigate the green dot to the right side. Maybe the goal is to arrange the colored blocks on the left side in a specific pattern.
Looking at the left side:
- Gray box at [30,8]-[33,11]
- Red bar at [30,12]-[33,17]
- Dark red box at [30,18]-[33,21]
- Blue block at [34,8]-[37,11]
- Gray box at [38,8]-[43,13]
And on the right side:
- Red cross target at [17,42]-[21,54]
- Blue cross target at [34,42]-[38,54]
Maybe the goal is to match the colors on the left side to the targets on the right side. The red target is at the top, and the blue target is at the bottom. On the left side, the red bar is at the top (rows 30-33), and the blue block is at the bottom (rows 34-37).
Wait, but the red bar and blue block are already in the correct positions relative to the targets. The red bar is at rows 30-33, and the red target is at rows 17-21. The blue block is at rows 34-37, and the blue target is at rows 34-38.
Hmm, maybe the goal is to move the green dot to the red bar to "activate" it, and then the red target on the right side gets filled. Similarly for the blue block.
But the green dot is currently in the dark red box, not the red bar. Let me move the green dot LEFT to enter the red bar.
Actually, wait. Let me look at the image more carefully. The green dot is at [30,20]-[31,21], which is at the top of the dark red box. The red bar is at [30,12]-[33,17], which is to the left of the dark red box. If I move the green dot LEFT, it should enter the red bar.
Let me try moving the green dot LEFT to enter the red bar, and see if anything happens.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model updated:
- Green dot at [30,20]-[31,21], blocked from moving UP (top of dark red box)
- Green dot can only move LEFT (into red bar) or DOWN (within dark red box)
- Green dot seems trapped in dark red box, can't reach right side
- Need to reconsider goal - maybe need to visit specific colored blocks to "activate" them
Plan: Move LEFT into red bar to see if it triggers something

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:

<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Move green dot LEFT into red bar
action(['LEFT', 'LEFT', 'LEFT', 'LEFT', 'LEFT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
